content.full_text:
City of Fremont
City Attorney's Office
39700 Civic Center Drive
P.O. Box 5006
Fremont, California 94537

(415) 745-2730

March 14, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box B07
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Request for Written Advice on Conflict of Interest Question

Gentlepersons:

The City of Fremont is requesting formal written advice as to potential conflict of interest disqualifications of two City of Fremont officials in decisions relating to the adoption of a specific plan for Fremont's Centerville District. The two officials on whose behalf this request is made are Mayor Bill Ball and Planning Commissioner Pauline McIvor. Mayor Ball and his wife own and reside in a single family detached house in the Centerville area. Planning Commissioner McIvor and her husband own two single family houses in the Centerville area—both of which they rent to residential tenants.

I have done some brief research, and it seems to me that the facts pertaining to this inquiry closely parallel those set out in Commission's Owen Opinion, 2 FPPC 77. Under the holding of that opinion, I believe that neither Mayor Ball nor Planning Commissioner McIvor is prohibited from participating in decisions relating to the Centerville specific plan. This is because implementation of the specific plan will affect the economic interests of each of them, if at all, in substantially the same manner that the economic interests of numerous other homeowners constituting a significant segment of the public will be affected. Also, there is no indication that any aspect of the specific plan will have any peculiar identifiable effect on the property of either official. The facts on which my tentative legal conclusion is based are set forth below:

Fremont has a population of more than one hundred seventy thousand, and the Centerville District is one of the original five towns which jointly incorporated to form Fremont in 1956. Centerville has experienced slow economic growth for a number of years due to the location of most new retail, service, and office facilities in other districts and in nearby shopping malls.

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 14, 1990
Page Two


Decline of the area has been minimized because of the success of
several new car dealerships which dominate Centerville's
commercial area. Most of these new car dealers have expressed
interest in relocating to an auto mall to be built far from the
Centerville District and on the west side of I-880 in Fremont.
The main objectives of the Centerville specific plan are to
prepare a land use plan for reuse of the auto dealer sites and
other vacant and underutilized land, and to adopt strategies for
economic revitalization of the area.

Fremont has designed a Centerville survey area consisting of
approximately 40 square blocks plus frontage along three miles of
Fremont Boulevard along about one mile of Thornton Avenue and
along one and one-third miles of Central Avenue. A study area
was later defined which encompasses about 80 percent of the
survey area. The survey area and the survey area are both part of
the Centerville Planning area. See attached map. In the study
area there are more than three hundred single family homes
generally similar to those owned by Mayor Ball and Commissioner
McIvor. In the survey area the number of similar homes is
greater than seven hundred. The planning area contains
approximately 10,000 single family homes.

Although successful revitalization of the Centerville
Business District would have a salutary affect on the whole
Centerville Planning Area (larger than both the study and the
survey area), actual specific plan land use changes are likely to
be limited to parcels meeting one or more of the following
criteria:

·    land and/or buildings currently vacant

·    auto dealer or auto-related use

·    underutilized land (built space significantly less than
     allowed by zoning)

·    obsolete design/significant lack of maintenance and
     investment

These criteria have been applied principally in the
commercial business district. In general, there have been no
land use changes recommended in any established residential
neighborhood. The three parcels in question (Commissioner
McIvor's two rental houses on Elm, and Mayor Ball's home on
Hamsett) would be considered to be established residential
areas where land use changes are not contemplated. It should
also be noted that Mayor Ball's home is within the survey area
but outside the study area in which major land use changes are
being proposed.

Fair Political Practices Commission
March 14, 1990
Page Three


     The commercial core of Centerville is surrounded by single
family homes and apartments.  The planning work completed so far
(three concept plans) would suggest that, while certain aspects
of the proposed plans may operate to increase the value and
rental value of the McIvor and Ball properties, implementation of
the plans would have a "substantially similar" effect on numerous
other residential properties in and near the survey and study
areas and probably, but to a far lesser degree, throughout the
entire planning area.

     As required by Section 18329 of the Regulations, please be
advised the mailing addresses of Mayor Ball and Commissioner
McIvor are as follows:

          Bill Ball
          4825 Hansen Avenue
          Fremont, CA 94536

          Pauline McIvor
          46281 Klamath Street
          Fremont, CA 94539

     Also as required by Section 18329, please be informed that both
Mayor Ball and Commissioner McIvor have given their authorization
that the City Attorney's office direct this inquiry to you.

                                        Sincerely yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        DALE L. LOPUS
                                        Assistant City Attorney

cc: Mayor Ball
    Commissioner McIvor

STUDY AREA

CENTERVILLE PLANNING AREA

CENTERVILLE SURVEY AREA

FIGURE 2

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Bill Ball and Planning Commissioner Pauline McIvor are prohibited from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of a specific plan for Fremont's Centerville District due to potential conflicts of interest, given their property ownership in the area.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request from City of Fremont to FPPC, not an FPPC response. The document contains the City Attorney's legal analysis and tentative conclusion, but does not constitute an official FPPC advice letter. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; question and conclusion synthesized from body text. Document includes factual background about the Centerville District and the survey/study areas affected by the proposed plan.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether Mayor Bill Ball and Planning Commissioner Pauline McIvor are prohibited from participating in decisions regarding the adoption of a specific plan for Fremont's Centerville District due to potential conflicts of interest, given their property ownership in the area.

embedding.summary: The City of Fremont's City Attorney requests formal written advice from the FPPC regarding potential conflict of interest disqualifications for Mayor Bill Ball and Planning Commissioner Pauline McIvor in decisions relating to a Centerville District specific plan. The City Attorney argues that neither official should be disqualified because the plan's effects on their properties would be substantially similar to effects on numerous other homeowners in the area, citing the FPPC's Owen opinion.
